mnemonics.title: BGP Message Types
mnemonics.content: O-U-K-N: Old Uncles Keep Napping - OPEN, UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, NOTIFICATION

mnemonics.title: Path Attribute Categories
mnemonics.content: WVA: Well-known Mandatory (AS-PATH, NEXT-HOP), Well-known Discretionary (LOCAL-PREF), Optional Transitive (COMMUNITY), Optional Non-transitive (MED)

mnemonics.title: Route Selection Preference
mnemonics.content: HIGH LOW: Highest LOCAL-PREF first, then lowest AS-PATH length, then lowest MED, then eBGP over iBGP, then lowest IGP cost to NEXT-HOP

acronyms.usage: A collection of routers under single administrative control
The primary path vector protocol used for inter-domain routing on the Internet
Attribute containing sequence of AS numbers that a route has traversed; used for loop detection
IP address of the next-hop router to reach the destination
BGP attribute indicating preference for outbound traffic; higher value = more preferred
BGP attribute to influence incoming traffic from neighboring AS; lower value = more preferred
BGP peering between routers in different Autonomous Systems
BGP peering between routers within the same Autonomous System

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circular track with AS numbers as checkpoints. When your own AS number appears in the path, you've gone in a circle - reject the route!
Draw 6 circles connected by arrows: Idle → Connect → Active → OpenSent → OpenConfirm → Established. Show what triggers each transition.
Create a decision tree: Is route valid? → Highest LOCAL-PREF? → Shortest AS-PATH? → Lowest MED? → eBGP? → Lowest IGP cost? → Lowest BGP ID?

analogies.analogy: Distance Vector is like asking neighbors for directions to reach a destination (blind trust). Path Vector is like getting a complete travel itinerary showing all cities visited - you can spot if you're going in circles.
Think of BGP as a country having customs officers at borders. They can choose which roads to allow traffic through based on political agreements, not just shortest path.
AS numbers are like postal codes for countries/regions. Each router within an AS shares the same postal code, and routers outside know how to route to any address within that code.
eBGP is like international border crossings where passports are checked (different AS). iBGP is like domestic highways within one country where no border checks occur but rules still apply.
